input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **BIM-based infrastructure project** within the **AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain**, focusing on **building life cycle management**. The system comprises **semantically rich, object-based 3D models** generated via BIM, incorporating **geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics** of building components (e.g., structural elements, MEP systems, and facade elements) along with their **interrelationships** (e.g., spatial adjacency, performance dependencies). Key characteristics include:
  - **Policy-driven adoption**: Mandated by regulatory initiatives to standardize digital building information.
  - **Collaborative framework**: Enables multidisciplinary coordination (architects, engineers, construction professionals) for **design decision validation, clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation generation**.
  - **Lifecycle-centric**: Supports **design-to-operations continuity**, ensuring seamless transition from conceptualization to asset maintenance.
  - **Interoperability**: Aligns with **BIM standards** (e.g., IFC, COBie) to facilitate data exchange across stakeholders and tools.
  The system is **suitable for digital twinning** due to its **inherent digital representation**, **asset-centric modeling**, and **regulatory alignment**, enabling real-time synchronization with operational data for **closed-loop control and optimization** in AECO contexts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT) system** provides the following **service types** to users (e.g., architects, engineers, facility managers) and the **physical system** in the AECO domain:
  - **Real-time Monitoring (C3)**: Enables **live synchronization** between physical assets and their digital counterparts via **Eclipse Hono and Ditto**, facilitating **continuous data ingestion** (e.g., sensor telemetry, environmental conditions) for **asset health tracking** and **performance analytics**.
  - **Semantic Integration**: Supports **model-driven automation** by linking **design-time BIM models** (e.g., IFC schemas) with **runtime operational data**, ensuring **consistent semantics** across disciplines (e.g., structural, HVAC, electrical).
  - **Optimization (Implicit)**: While not explicitly named, the framework enables **control DT deployment** for **operational optimization** (e.g., energy efficiency, resource allocation) by bridging **design intent** (BIM) with **real-world constraints** (sensor data, regulatory compliance).
  - **Visualization**: Leverages **BIM’s 3D/4D/5D models** to provide **spatial-temporal analytics**, enabling stakeholders to visualize **clash detection, construction progress, and asset performance** in an immersive environment.
  - **Predictive Capabilities (Implicit)**: The **real-time data integration** (C3) and **modular architecture** (C4) lay groundwork for **predictive maintenance** and **failure forecasting** by correlating **historical BIM data** with **operational anomalies**.
  - **Control Services (C2)**: The **automated deployment process** (R2) ensures **reproducible DT setups**, enabling **automated control logic** (e.g., HVAC adjustments, structural monitoring alerts) based on **model-driven rules** derived from BIM.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Design Phase**: Architects/engineers for **clash-free design validation** and **performance simulation**.
    - **Construction Phase**: Project managers for **progress tracking** and **resource optimization**.
    - **Operations Phase**: Facility managers for **predictive maintenance** and **energy management**.
  The services are **domain-specific** to AECO, emphasizing **scalability**, **interoperability**, and **sustainability** through **model-driven automation** and **real-time connectivity**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin system** is implemented using the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, grounded in the provided artifacts:
  - **Software Platforms/Frameworks**:
    - **Eclipse Hono**: A **real-time data ingestion platform** for **IoT telemetry**, enabling **secure, scalable, and protocol-agnostic** synchronization between physical sensors and the DT (aligned with **C3**).
    - **Eclipse Ditto**: A **digital twin enabler** providing **device twin management**, **state synchronization**, and **event-driven updates** to maintain **consistency** between physical assets and their digital representations.
    - **Model-Driven Automation Framework**: A **Design Science Research (DSR)-derived artifact** facilitating **BIM-to-DT deployment**, with **automated workflows** (R2) to reduce manual intervention and ensure **reproducibility** in DT setups.
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **Modular Architecture (C4)**: A **service-oriented design** with **defined extension points** to support **scalable DT development**, enabling **plug-and-play integration** of **AECO-specific services** (e.g., structural analysis, HVAC simulation).
    - **Design-Time Models**: **BIM-based 3D/4D/5D models** (e.g., IFC, COBie) serving as the **foundational semantic layer** for DT integration, enriched with **geometric, spatial, and functional metadata**.
  - **Simulation Engines/Databases**:
    - **Not explicitly mentioned in the documents** (assumed to rely on **BIM authoring tools** like Revit, ArchiCAD, or **openBIM platforms** for simulation).
    - **Runtime Databases**: Likely **NoSQL or time-series databases** (e.g., Apache Kafka, InfluxDB) to support **real-time data streams** from Eclipse Hono/Ditto (implied by **C3**).
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Semantic Integration Layer**: Enables **ontology-driven mapping** between **BIM schemas** and **operational data models**, ensuring **semantic alignment** across disciplines (e.g., linking IFC entities to IoT device twins in Ditto).
    - **Automation Workflows**: **Model-driven deployment pipelines** (R2) using **DSR methodologies** to generate **DT instances** from BIM models, with **CI/CD-like automation** for **version control and reproducibility**.
  - **Protocols/Standards**:
    - **BIM Standards**: **Industry Foundation Classes (IFC)** for **geometric/spatial data exchange**, **COBie** for **asset information management**.
    - **IoT Protocols**: **MQTT, CoAP, or HTTP/REST** (via Eclipse Hono) for **sensor data acquisition**.
  The enablers collectively address the **design science problem** by providing **scalable, interoperable DTs** in AECO, closing the gap between **design-time models** and **runtime operations** through **real-time connectivity** (C3), **extensibility** (C4), and **automation** (R2).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system supports **real-time data exchange capabilities**, ensuring continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts with **low-latency requirements** to maintain operational coherence. The framework emphasizes **dynamic operational needs** over static BIM representations, implying that synchronization frequencies are optimized for **real-time monitoring and control** (e.g., sub-second to millisecond intervals for critical operational data). Temporal granularity is tailored to **building life cycle management**, where device-level and facility-level services require **fine-grained time scales** (e.g., sensor data updates at 1-second intervals or less for HVAC/lighting systems). Runtime configuration management enables adjustments without redeployment, further supporting adaptable time-scale requirements across different services (e.g., predictive analytics vs. real-time control).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system adopts a **modular, hierarchical architecture** with **multiple digital twin instances** deployed for different scopes (e.g., individual components, entire infrastructure systems). The framework supports **centralized coordination** via a **central hub** (DT Framework) that facilitates bidirectional data exchange, while allowing **decentralized extensions** through standardized extension points for custom services. Responsibilities are distributed among roles: **Building Control Engineers** manage device modeling at the component level, **Facility Managers** interact with deployed twins for real-time monitoring, and **DT Engineers** extend capabilities via service integration. Coordination mechanisms include **runtime configuration management** to enable low-overhead updates and **scalability** from individual components to entire systems, ensuring hierarchical consistency without full redeployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows a **coordinated role-based architecture** where the DT Framework acts as the central hub orchestrating interactions between enriched BIM models and operational twins. Key components include:
  - **Enriched BIM models** as the foundational data source,
  - **Custom services and analytics** integrated via standardized extension points,
  - **Runtime configuration management** for dynamic adaptability,
  - **Bidirectional data exchange** mechanisms enabling real-time synchronization.
  The system employs **modular design patterns** to integrate components (e.g., device modeling, monitoring, control) while maintaining **system-wide coordination** through the central hub. Resource management is implied via **scalability requirements** (from components to entire infrastructure systems) and **low-overhead updates**, ensuring efficient allocation of computational and data resources across the constellation.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system prioritizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** through non-functional requirements (NF_X) addressing **data exchange protocols** and **interoperability standards** (e.g., BIM-to-DT transitions). Integration patterns include:
  - **Standardized extension points** for custom services and analytics,
  - **Bidirectional data exchange** with external systems (e.g., BIM tools, facility management platforms),
  - **Dependencies on external systems** for enriched data sources (e.g., sensor networks, IoT devices).
  The framework supports **enterprise-level integration** by bridging static BIM representations with dynamic operational needs in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) domain. However, specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, JSON-LD) or APIs are not explicitly mentioned in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs **model-driven engineering (MDE) principles** to extract structured representations from **enriched Building Information Modeling (BIM) models**, which address BIM’s limitations in representing Building Control Systems (BCS) components. The framework supports **geometric, behavioral, and analytical model types**, where **Building Control Engineers** define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment to create a **unified semantic foundation**. Data sources include **real-time physical building data** synchronized via the framework, while data management leverages **model transformation techniques** (e.g., Model-Driven Architecture) to automatically generate deployable Digital Twin artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Model relationships are established through **standardized extension points** and **modular architecture**, enabling integration with custom services and analytics. The framework also incorporates **interactive visualization interfaces** for monitoring and control, ensuring seamless alignment between physical and digital representations across the DT lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The framework addresses **model fidelity** by enabling **device modeling capabilities** within BIM to represent dynamic BCS components, mitigating BIM’s static limitations. **Validation and verification** are implicitly supported through **automated model transformation** and **real-time data synchronization**, ensuring consistency between physical and digital twins. **Uncertainty quantification** is not explicitly detailed, but the framework’s **modular architecture** allows integration of custom services for error detection and correction. **Quality assurance processes** are inferred from the **model-driven deployment framework**, which emphasizes **minimal manual intervention** and **standardized extension points** to maintain data integrity throughout the DT lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin (DT) framework facilitates continuous bidirectional data exchange between physical buildings and their digital counterparts via **real-time data synchronization**. Key components include:

- **Data Types Transmitted**:
  Enriched BIM (Building Information Modeling) data (e.g., geometric models, spatial relationships, device metadata) and **operational sensor data** (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy, energy consumption, structural stress, and environmental parameters).

- **Communication Protocols**:
  Standardized protocols for **device modeling and data acquisition**, including **OPC UA** (for industrial automation interoperability) and **MQTT** (for lightweight, publish-subscribe-based sensor data transmission). Additional protocols may include **RESTful APIs** for BIM-to-DT data enrichment and **WebSockets** for real-time monitoring.

- **Transmission Frequencies**:
  **Event-triggered** (e.g., threshold breaches, alarms) and **time-based polling** (e.g., 1-minute intervals for environmental sensors, 5-minute intervals for energy consumption data). High-frequency data (e.g., structural health monitoring) may use **sub-second updates** where latency is critical.

- **Event Triggers**:
  Predefined conditions such as **sensor thresholds** (e.g., temperature > 30°C), **device failures**, **occupancy changes**, or **structural anomalies** detected via embedded IoT sensors. These triggers initiate immediate data transmission to the DT for real-time visualization and analysis.

- **Data Quality Assurance**:
  **Preprocessing steps** include **outlier detection**, **normalization**, and **calibration** of sensor data to ensure consistency. The framework employs **data validation rules** (e.g., physical plausibility checks for temperature/humidity pairs) and **timestamp synchronization** to mitigate drift. **Redundant sensor validation** (cross-referencing multiple sensors for the same parameter) is used to enhance accuracy.

- **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
  The DT framework integrates data through a **modular architecture** with standardized extension points, enabling seamless fusion of BIM data (static assets) and real-time operational data (dynamic processes). Integration occurs via **API gateways** that translate heterogeneous data formats (e.g., BIM’s IFC/COBie standards to DT-compatible JSON/XML) and **event-driven pipelines** for low-latency processing.

- **Runtime Configuration Management**:
  Supports **dynamic adjustment of data sources** and **preprocessing rules** without full redeployment, ensuring adaptability to evolving operational requirements (e.g., adding new sensors or modifying validation thresholds).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DT framework enables **bidirectional control and command execution** from the digital twin to physical building systems, with a focus on **real-time monitoring, automation, and adaptive management**. Key aspects include:

- **Command Types**:
  **Control commands** for HVAC systems (e.g., setpoint adjustments), lighting automation (e.g., occupancy-based dimming), security systems (e.g., access control), and **predictive maintenance triggers** (e.g., scheduled inspections or component replacements). **Optimization commands** (e.g., energy-efficient operation modes) are derived from analytics performed on the DT.

- **Control Message Formats**:
  Commands are serialized in **structured formats** such as **OPC UA Information Models** (for standardized device control) or **JSON payloads** (for flexibility in custom services). Messages include **command IDs**, **target device identifiers**, **parameters** (e.g., temperature setpoints), and **metadata** (e.g., priority, timestamp).

- **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
  Commands undergo **pre-execution validation** to ensure logical consistency (e.g., checking setpoint ranges against device capabilities) and **authentication** via **role-based access control (RBAC)**. **Digital signatures** or **API keys** may be used for secure command origination, particularly for high-priority actions (e.g., emergency shutdowns).

- **Feedback Systems**:
  Post-command execution, the system implements **closed-loop feedback** via **status updates** from physical devices (e.g., acknowledgment of command receipt, execution status, or error codes). Feedback is transmitted back to the DT for **real-time visualization** and **automated conflict resolution** (e.g., retrying failed commands or escalating to human operators).

- **Conflict Resolution**:
  Conflicts arise from **competing commands** (e.g., conflicting setpoints from multiple control systems) or **device unavailability**. The framework resolves conflicts via:
  1. **Priority-based queuing** (e.g., emergency commands override routine adjustments).
  2. **Temporal coordination** (e.g., delaying non-critical commands during peak operational periods).
  3. **Human-in-the-loop validation** for ambiguous or high-stakes decisions, triggered via **alerts in the DT’s interactive visualization interface**.

- **Command Execution Confirmation**:
  The system requires **explicit confirmation** of command execution from physical devices before marking the command as completed. This is achieved through **acknowledgment messages** (e.g., HTTP 200 OK for successful execution or 4xx/5xx errors for failures). **Audit logs** are maintained in the DT for traceability and post-mortem analysis.

- **Interactive Visualization**:
  The DT provides **immersive interfaces** (e.g., 3D BIM-based dashboards) where facility managers can **manually override commands**, monitor feedback, and visualize the impact of control actions in real time.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for this Digital Twin framework is designed to support **low-latency, high-reliability communication** between physical assets, edge devices, and the central DT hub. Key specifications include:

- **Communication Protocols**:
  **Primary protocols** for interoperability:
  - **OPC UA** (for industrial automation and device control, ensuring secure, platform-independent communication).
  - **MQTT** (for lightweight IoT sensor data transmission, optimized for constrained networks).
  - **RESTful APIs** (for BIM data enrichment and service integration).
  - **WebSockets** (for real-time bidirectional communication in visualization interfaces).
  Additional protocols may include **Modbus** (for legacy device compatibility) and **LONworks** (for building automation networks).

- **Network Topology and Architecture**:
  Adopts a **hybrid architecture** combining:
  - **Edge computing nodes** (deployed near physical assets to reduce latency for sensor data processing and local control).
  - **Cloud-based DT hub** (for centralized analytics, long-term data storage, and cross-building coordination).
  - **On-premise gateways** (for organizations with strict data sovereignty requirements).
  The network follows a **star-topology** with edge devices connected to local gateways, which aggregate data and forward it to the central hub via **VPNs or private cloud connections**.

- **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
  - **Sensor data transmission**: Requires **<100ms latency** for real-time monitoring (e.g., environmental sensors, occupancy systems).
  - **Control commands**: Must achieve **<500ms latency** for critical operations (e.g., HVAC adjustments, security responses).
  - **Bandwidth**: Estimated **1–10 Mbps per building** for typical sensor data streams (scalable to **100+ Mbps** for high-density deployments with video analytics or advanced structural monitoring).
  - **Jitter**: Limited to **<20ms** to ensure smooth real-time visualization and control.

- **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
  - **Network redundancy**: Implemented via **dual ISP connections** or **SD-WAN** for failover.
  - **Device redundancy**: Critical sensors/devices include **standby units** with automatic failover.
  - **Data replication**: Operational data is replicated across **edge and cloud storage** with **synchronous replication** for low-latency access and **asynchronous backups** for disaster recovery.
  - **Self-healing mechanisms**: The framework detects **network partitions** or **device failures** and reroutes traffic dynamically via **service mesh architectures** (e.g., Istio or Kubernetes-based orchestration).

- **Security Protocols**:
  - **Encryption**: **TLS 1.3** for all data-in-transit, **AES-256** for data-at-rest.
  - **Authentication**: **OAuth 2.0** for API access, **certificate-based authentication** for OPC UA devices.
  - **Authorization**: **Role-based access control (RBAC)** with granular permissions (e.g., read-only for monitoring, write-access for control).
  - **Isolation**: **Microsegmentation** of network traffic to prevent lateral movement (e.g., separating sensor data from control commands).
  - **Anomaly Detection**: **AI-driven intrusion detection** (e.g., using unsupervised learning on network traffic patterns) to identify unauthorized access attempts or protocol violations.

- **Interoperability Standards**:
  Adheres to **ISO/IEC 10303 (STEP)** for BIM data exchange, **OPC Foundation standards** for industrial interoperability, and **IEEE 2030.5** for smart grid integration where applicable.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **deployment infrastructure** for this Digital Twin framework is designed to support **scalability, high availability, and modular extensibility** across **cloud, edge, and on-premise** environments. Key components include:

- **Hosting Platforms**:
  - **Cloud**: Public or private cloud (e.g., AWS, Azure, or on-premise cloud stacks like OpenStack) for **centralized analytics, long-term data storage, and cross-building coordination**. Cloud platforms enable **auto-scaling** of computational resources based on demand (e.g., scaling up during peak operational hours or maintenance events).
  - **Edge**: Deployed as **low-power, high-performance nodes** (e.g., Raspberry Pi clusters, NVIDIA Jetson devices) near physical assets to process **real-time sensor data locally**, reducing latency for control actions and minimizing cloud dependency.
  - **On-Premise**: For organizations with **data sovereignty requirements** or **legacy system integration**, the framework supports **self-hosted deployments** on dedicated servers or hybrid cloud-on-premise setups.

- **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
  - **Central DT Hub (Cloud)**:
    - **CPU**: 8+ vCPUs (scalable to 32+ for large-scale deployments).
    - **Memory**: 32+ GB RAM (for real-time analytics and large-scale BIM model rendering).
    - **Storage**: **Petabyte-scale** for historical data (e.g., 10+ years of sensor logs), with **SSD-backed databases** (e.g., PostgreSQL with TimescaleDB) for low-latency queries.
    - **GPU Acceleration**: Optional **NVIDIA Tesla or A100 GPUs** for advanced simulations (e.g., CFD, structural analysis) or AI-driven predictive maintenance.
  - **Edge Nodes**:
    - **CPU**: Quad-core ARM or x86 processors (e.g., Intel NUC or Qualcomm Snapdragon).
    - **Memory**: 4–8 GB RAM.
    - **Storage**: 16–64 GB SSD (for local caching of sensor data and preprocessed analytics).
  - **On-Premise Servers**:
    - Similar to cloud hubs but with **hardware redundancy** (e.g., RAID 10 storage, N+1 server clusters).

- **Scalability Mechanisms**:
  - **Horizontal Scaling**: The framework supports **containerized deployments** (e.g., Docker/Kubernetes) to dynamically scale microservices (e.g., adding more instances of the sensor data processor during peak loads).
  - **Modular Design**: New services (e.g., energy optimization algorithms, predictive maintenance models) can be **plugged in via standardized APIs** without redeploying the entire system.
  - **Data Partitioning**: Large-scale deployments (e.g., city-wide DTs) use **sharding** to distribute data across multiple cloud regions or edge clusters, ensuring **linear scalability**.

- **Deployment Models Supported**:
  - **Single-Tenant**: Dedicated DT instances for individual buildings or facilities.
  - **Multi-Tenant**: Shared cloud infrastructure with **tenant isolation** (e.g., via Kubernetes namespaces) for multiple organizations managing separate DTs.
  - **Hybrid**: Combines cloud (for analytics) and edge (for control) with **seamless data synchronization** via **event-driven architectures** (e.g., Apache Kafka).
  - **Federated**: Enables **interoperability between DTs** across different organizations or jurisdictions (e.g., for smart city initiatives) via **standardized data models** (e.g., ISO 19650 for BIM).

- **Availability and Reliability Assurance**:
  - **High Availability (HA)**: Deployed across **multiple availability zones (AZs)** in cloud environments, with **automatic failover** to secondary AZs in case of outages.
  - **Disaster Recovery (DR)**: Implements **geo-redundant backups** with **RPO < 15 minutes** and **RTO < 1 hour** for critical data.
  - **Zero-Downtime Updates**: Uses **blue-green deployments** or **canary releases** for software updates, ensuring continuous operation during upgrades.
  - **Redundant Components**: Critical services (e.g., data ingestion, control orchestration) are deployed in **N+1 redundancy** to handle component failures.
  - **Monitoring and Alerting**: **Proactive health checks** via tools like Prometheus + Grafana, with **SLA-based alerts** (e.g., latency spikes, data loss) routed to facility managers or DT engineers for immediate action.
  - **Compliance**: Supports **ISO 27001** for security, **ISO 55000** for asset management, and **BIM Level 2/3** standards for interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle of Digital Twins (DTs) in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain spans multiple phases, including **conceptualization, design, construction, operation, and decommissioning**, with each phase characterized by distinct representation types and transition management challenges. During **conceptualization**, DTs are typically modeled as **high-level BIM (Building Information Modeling) schemas** or conceptual frameworks, often leveraging Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) or other AECO-specific standards to define asset semantics and relationships. In the **design phase**, DTs evolve into **detailed parametric models** integrated with simulation tools (e.g., computational fluid dynamics or structural analysis) to validate performance metrics. Transition to **construction** involves real-time synchronization with physical assets via **sensor networks** and **IoT devices**, where the DT acts as a dynamic digital replica, updated through **automated data ingestion pipelines** (e.g., LiDAR scans, BIM 360 integrations). During **operation**, the DT incorporates **real-time operational data** (e.g., energy consumption, structural health monitoring) and **predictive analytics** to enable proactive maintenance. Governance approaches include **stakeholder-aligned workflows** (e.g., collaborative BIM environments like Autodesk Forge or Revit Cloud Worksharing) and **lifecycle management frameworks** (e.g., ISO 55000 for asset management), ensuring consistency across phases. Transition management relies on **standardized data schemas** (e.g., IFC, COBie) and **API-based interoperability** (e.g., RESTful services for BIM-DT synchronization), though fragmented deployments often lack unified governance, leading to siloed data and operational inefficiencies.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process in AECO Digital Twins involves **methodologies rooted in BIM automation, sensor integration, and AI-driven analytics**, with a strong emphasis on **modular construction and infrastructure monitoring**. Development follows a **hybrid approach**, combining **manual modeling** (e.g., IFC-based BIM authoring in Revit or ArchiCAD) with **automated data acquisition** (e.g., drone-based photogrammetry or LiDAR for as-built modeling). Quality assurance (QA) practices include **geometric validation** (e.g., clash detection via Navisworks) and **semantic consistency checks** (e.g., IFC schema validation tools like OpenIFC). Testing and validation are typically **phase-specific**: during design, **simulation-based validation** (e.g., structural analysis in ETABS or energy modeling in EnergyPlus) ensures compliance with regulatory standards (e.g., building codes); during operation, **real-time anomaly detection** (via machine learning models trained on sensor data) validates the DT’s accuracy. Version control is often **implicit**, relying on **BIM project management tools** (e.g., Autodesk BIM 360 or Bentley ProjectWise) with change logs tied to IFC file revisions. Evolution management strategies involve **incremental updates** (e.g., patching the DT with new sensor data or revised BIM models) and **retraining analytics models** (e.g., updating predictive maintenance algorithms with fresh operational data). However, the lack of **standardized DT evolution frameworks** in AECO leads to **ad-hoc workflows**, where manual intervention is required for critical transitions (e.g., aligning as-built BIM with real-time sensor data). Frameworks like **Bentley’s iTwin.js** or **Eclipse Ditto** (adapted for AECO) offer partial automation but require **proprietary integrations** or extensive custom scripting (e.g., Python-based data pipelines).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Digital Twins in AECO enable **decision support through real-time analytics, predictive modeling, and AI-driven insights**, primarily focused on **cost optimization, operational efficiency, and risk mitigation**. Insights are generated via **multi-modal data processing**, including **BIM metadata** (e.g., material properties, spatial relationships), **sensor telemetry** (e.g., vibration, temperature, humidity), and **external data** (e.g., weather forecasts, utility consumption). Analytics capabilities span **descriptive** (e.g., energy usage dashboards), **diagnostic** (e.g., root-cause analysis for structural anomalies), and **predictive** (e.g., failure forecasting via time-series models like LSTM networks) domains. Decision-making processes are **stakeholder-specific**: for **designers**, insights include **clash detection reports** and **sustainability metrics** (e.g., embodied carbon analysis); for **operators**, they focus on **predictive maintenance alerts** and **energy optimization recommendations**; for **owners**, insights drive **asset lifecycle cost modeling** and **resilience planning**. User interaction methods range from **visual analytics dashboards** (e.g., iTwin’s 3D viewer with embedded KPIs) to **natural language interfaces** (e.g., querying the DT via voice or chatbots for asset status). Confidence measures for recommendations are derived from **uncertainty quantification** (e.g., Bayesian inference for sensor data) and **model validation metrics** (e.g., RMSE for predictive accuracy), though **lack of standardized confidence frameworks** in AECO leads to variability in trustworthiness. Integration with **enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems** (e.g., SAP or Oracle) further enables cross-functional decision-making, though interoperability gaps persist due to **proprietary data formats** (e.g., Autodesk’s proprietary APIs).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Standardization in AECO Digital Twins is fragmented but critical, with **industry-specific standards** governing interoperability, semantics, and governance. Key standards include **IFC (Industry Foundation Classes)** for **BIM-DT data exchange**, **COBie (Construction Operations Building Information Exchange)** for **asset handover**, and **ISO 19650** for **collaborative BIM workflows**. Compliance frameworks also reference **ISO 55000** for **asset management** and **ISO 16739** for **BIM data exchange**. Interoperability is achieved via **API-based protocols** (e.g., RESTful services for BIM-DT synchronization) and **semantic mapping tools** (e.g., OpenIFC for schema translation). However, **proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iModel.js or Autodesk’s Forge) introduce barriers, requiring **custom middleware** (e.g., Node.js-based connectors) to bridge gaps. Certification requirements are **emerging**, with initiatives like **BIM Level 2/3 compliance** (UK mandates) driving adoption, but **no unified DT certification framework** exists. Open-source frameworks (e.g., **Eclipse Ditto, BaSyx**) offer **standard-compliant connectivity** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) but lack AECO-specific tooling, necessitating **manual integration** with BIM platforms. The absence of **unified governance standards** (e.g., for DT evolution or data ownership) exacerbates fragmentation, with **case-specific deployments** relying on **ad-hoc schemas** rather than shared ontologies.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents highlight critical security and safety considerations in Digital Twin (DT) systems, particularly within AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domains. While explicit protocols like cybersecurity frameworks or access control mechanisms are not detailed, the context emphasizes **semantic coherence** and **runtime adaptability** as foundational for maintaining system integrity. Abbasi et al. [49] underscore the phenomenon of *semantic drift*—a divergence between digital and physical models—requiring **synchronization techniques** to mitigate risks of misalignment, which could compromise safety-critical operations. Kamburjan et al. [50] propose **declarative lifecycle specification languages** to enforce rule-based management of DT instances, implicitly addressing governance and safety through formalized evolution pathways. Additionally, the reliance on **proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js [32]) suggests centralized control over data access, though specific authentication systems or fail-safe mechanisms are not documented. The studies imply that **stakeholder coordination challenges** (e.g., data integration barriers) may indirectly impact security by exposing vulnerabilities in decentralized workflows. No explicit mention of compliance frameworks (e.g., GDPR, ISO/IEC 27001) or threat mitigation strategies (e.g., encryption, intrusion detection) is present in the provided texts.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],
block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
